title: Dealing with an angry patient — delayed test results
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 GP trainee. Mr Keith Lambert, a 52-year-old lorry driver, has come to see you. He had a prostate biopsy 6 weeks ago and was told he would receive his results within 2 weeks. He has not received results and is extremely angry. You have the results: Gleason 3+3=6, organ-confined prostate cancer. Please manage this situation.
examinerNotes: De-escalation station combined with breaking bad news. Candidate must: acknowledge anger, apologise for delay (institutional not personal), not become defensive, de-escalate before delivering cancer diagnosis. Must use SPIKES after de-escalation. Critical error: defending the system, dismissing anger.

patient.name: Keith Lambert
patient.presentingComplaint: Furious about 6-week wait for biopsy results — came in demanding answers
patient.reasonForAttendance: I'm absolutely furious. Six weeks and nobody has bothered to contact me. I've been going out of my mind with worry.

hpi.character: Extremely angry, voice raised, arms crossed, pacing
associatedSymptoms: Has not told wife as didn't want to worry her
hpi.exacerbatingFactors: Being dismissed or made to feel unimportant

socialHistory.occupation: HGV driver — DVLA implications if cancer requires treatment

systemsReview.genitourinary: Lower urinary tract symptoms (slow stream, frequency) for 2 years — reason for PSA check

examination.general: Angry, standing, arms crossed, voice raised

patient.emotionalState: Angry initially. Once acknowledged: scared, vulnerable, worried about cancer. Will ask 'Is it cancer?'. If handled poorly will escalate and refuse to engage.
patient.communicationStyle: Initially confrontational. If doctor listens and apologises sincerely, he will gradually calm down. Underneath the anger is profound fear.
hiddenConcerns: My dad died from prostate cancer — I've been thinking about nothing else
I haven't slept properly for 6 weeks
My wife doesn't know I had the biopsy
What does this mean for my job — can I still drive?
doNotVolunteer: Father's death from prostate cancer — only if anger calms and doctor asks about family history or why he's so worried
Wife doesn't know — only if asked who is supporting him
Work concerns — only after anger calms and doctor asks about impact on his life

primaryDiagnosis: Gleason 3+3=6 (Grade Group 1) Organ-Confined Prostate Cancer — localised, low-risk

dataGathering.criterion: Allows patient to express anger without interrupting
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Listens actively, does not interrupt or become defensive
'I understand why you're angry' or equivalent — validates emotion

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Institutional apology — 'I am sorry this happened' without blaming individuals
Uses warning shot before delivering cancer diagnosis
States prostate cancer, Gleason low-grade, organ-confined — positive framing appropriate

clinicalManagement.criterion: Explains this is low-grade, treatable, often curable
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Reassures about low Gleason score, good prognosis, multidisciplinary plan

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Does not escalate or become defensive
Addresses underlying fear not just surface anger
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Recognises fear under anger, asks about patient's concerns

references.source: NICE NG131: Prostate cancer — diagnosis and management

learningObjectives: De-escalate an angry patient using active listening
Transition from de-escalation to breaking bad news
